Client: Yeah, that’s perfect. I don’t have time for a super long thing.
Advisor: Totally. Starting with income—what’s your household gross income per year right now, just you?
Client: My gross is 123300 a year.
Advisor: Okay. And is that mostly salary, or do you have other income streams too?
Client: It’s a mix. I’ve got investment income—interest and dividends—coming in different ways.

Client: The other is paid annually, and that one is 93700 a year.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. Switching to spending: what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in—housing, food, kids, everything?
Client: About 6800 a month.
Advisor: When you say 6800, does that include your mortgage payment, or is that separate?
Client: That includes it—sorry, I should’ve said that. The mortgage is part of the 6800.

Client: That includes it—sorry, I should’ve said that. The mortgage is part of the 6800.
Advisor: No worries. On the debt side then—what are your monthly debt payments total? If it’s just the mortgage, we’ll capture that.
Client: It’s just the mortgage, 2150 per month.
Advisor: Got it. And what’s the current mortgage balance outstanding?
Client: 104400 left on it.

Client: Yeah, the final payment date is 2031-06-11.
Advisor: Great. Now let’s talk about what you’ve built up—your investable assets total. What’s the ballpark across accounts that are investable?
Client: Total investable assets are 367300.
Advisor: Okay. I’m going to break that down so we know what’s where. Do you have a taxable investment account held through an insurance company, by chance?
Client: Yes—there’s a taxable account held at an insurance company, and it’s 216500.

Client: There is. Private markets held at an insurance company, 2800.
Advisor: And your home—your primary residence—what’s the current value you’re using?
Client: Primary residence value is 275600.
Advisor: Thanks. Last bucket: protection. Do you have any long-term care coverage in place?
Client: Yes, I have long-term care insurance.

Client: It’s just the mortgage, 2150 per month.
Advisor: Got it. And what’s the current mortgage balance outstanding?
Client: 104400 left on it.
Advisor: And the interest rate on that mortgage?
Client: 5.1%.

Client: 104400 left on it.

Client: 5.1%.

Client: Sounds good. I just want it organized and not a million moving parts.
Advisor: Totally hear you, Michael—organized and fewer moving parts. Before we wrap, I just need the household basics so I don’t assume anything.
Advisor: What’s your current marital status—are you single, married, divorced, something else?
Client: Divorced.
Advisor: Got it. And in the household day-to-day, how many adults are we talking—just you, or anyone else living with you?

Client: Yeah, exactly—my three kids.
Advisor: Perfect. Last one in this bucket: do you know what tax bracket you generally fall into right now? Even a band is fine.
Client: It’s 35%.
Advisor: Great—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything from earlier: income is that $123,300 gross plus the interest and dividends streams, assets include the $367,300 investable plus the home at $275,600, mortgage is the only debt at $104,400, and you’ve got the long-term care policy with the $150 monthly premium and $352,300 coverage through 2056-04-13.
Client: Yep, that’s the picture.

Advisor: And protection-wise, you’ve got long-term care insurance—premium 150 per month, coverage amount 352300, covered until 2056-04-13.
Client: Yep.
Advisor: Cool. Next time we’ll talk priorities—what you want the money to do first—so we can line up the aggressive risk tolerance with the “keep the family stable” part you mentioned earlier.
Client: Sounds good. I just want it organized and not a million moving parts.
Advisor: Totally hear you, Michael—organized and fewer moving parts. Before we wrap, I just need the household basics so I don’t assume anything.

Advisor: Got it. And for state, are you in one of the standard states we list, or should I mark it as Other?
Client: Put it as Other—literally Other.
Advisor: Okay, thanks. Next—how do you want to work together going forward? Are you looking for an RIA (registered investment advisor) relationship, or something more like a one-time plan?
Client: RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great. And just to make sure I’m not putting words in your mouth—when we talked about investing earlier, you said you’re comfortable taking risk, but you still want the kids’ baseline protected. How would you label your risk tolerance?
